lessons.title: Process User Data Into a Report
lessons.explanation: FDE work often starts with plain Python: receive messy records, normalize them, and explain what changed. In this lesson you will summarize a list of user dictionaries into a small report.
lessons.visual: raw users -> loop over dictionaries -> count active users -> collect emails -> return report

guided_exercise.title: Summarize users
guided_exercise.prompt: Open fde_api_academy/student/m01_python_report.py and implement summarize_users(users). Return total count, active count, and email list.

challenge_exercise.title: Handle missing optional fields
challenge_exercise.prompt: Update your implementation so optional keys do not crash the report. Missing active should count as False.

reflection_questions: What assumptions did your function make about input shape?
Which missing key would be most likely in real customer data?
hints: Start by creating a result dictionary with total, active, and emails.
Loop through each user and use user.get(...) for optional keys.
active should increase only when the value is truthy; append every email in order.
A solid solution returns {'total': len(users), 'active': sum(1 for u in users if u.get('active')), 'emails': [u.get('email') for u in users]}.

practice_drills.title: Normalize customer records
practice_drills.prompt: Write normalize_customer_records(records). Each record may have optional 'name', 'email', and 'active' keys. Return {'users': [...], 'incomplete': N} where every user is filled with defaults ('Unknown', '', False) and 'incomplete' counts records missing any of the three keys.
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Build a text report
practice_drills.prompt: Write build_text_report(records). Given customer records (each with an 'active' flag), return a three-line string: 'Total customers: X', 'Active customers: Y', 'Inactive customers: Z' separated by newlines.
practice_drills.source_title: Exponent Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.example: build_text_report([{'active': True}, {'active': False}, {'active': True}]) -> 'Total customers: 3\nActive customers: 2\nInactive customers: 1'
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

build_text_report([{'active': True}, {'active': False}, {'active': True}]) -> 'Total customers: 3\nActive customers: 2\nInactive customers: 1'

build_text_report([]) -> 'Total customers: 0\nActive customers: 0\nInactive customers: 0'

build_text_report([{'active': False}]) -> 'Total customers: 1\nActive customers: 0\nInactive customers: 1'

cases.expected:
Total customers: 3
Active customers: 2
Inactive customers: 1

Total customers: 0
Active customers: 0
Inactive customers: 0

Total customers: 1
Active customers: 0
Inactive customers: 1

practice_drills.title: Handle bad input safely
practice_drills.prompt: Write clean_records(records). Some items may not be dictionaries. Return {'users': [...only dict rows...], 'errors': N} where N counts the skipped non-dict rows.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks How to Parse Nested JSON in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Shape areas with ceiling
practice_drills.source_title: Palantir FDSE HackerRank 'Shape Classes' (reported)
practice_drills.verification_note: Modeled on a task reported by candidates for Palantir's Forward Deployed (Software) Engineer interview; verified against public write-ups on 2026-06-08.
practice_drills.prompt: Reported Palantir FDSE 'Shape Classes' HackerRank task. Write shape_areas(shapes). Each shape is {'type': 'circle'|'rectangle'|'square', ...dims} ('r' for circle, 'w'/'h' for rectangle, 'side' for square). Use pi = 3.14159265 and return the ceiling of each area as a list of ints.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

shape_areas([{'type': 'circle', 'r': 1.2}, {'type': 'square', 'side': 3}]) -> [5, 9]

shape_areas([{'type': 'rectangle', 'w': 2, 'h': 5}]) -> [10]

shape_areas([]) -> []

lessons.title: Traverse Nested API Data
lessons.explanation: APIs rarely return flat data. You need to move through nested dictionaries carefully, preserve useful fields, and survive missing optional values.

guided_exercise.title: Extract contacts
guided_exercise.prompt: Implement extract_contacts(payload). Return a list of dictionaries with name, email, and phone.

challenge_exercise.title: Preserve missing phone numbers
challenge_exercise.prompt: Make sure users without phone numbers return phone: None instead of crashing.

reflection_questions: Where would KeyError happen in this payload?
When should you use direct indexing instead of get()?
hints: Iterate over payload.get('users', []).
Read profile and contact as separate nested dictionaries.
Use .get('phone') so missing phone values become None.
Build one output row per user: {'name': profile.get('name'), 'email': contact.get('email'), 'phone': contact.get('phone')}.

practice_drills.title: Extract nested owner emails
practice_drills.prompt: Write extract_owner_emails(data) for data shaped like {'accounts': [{'owner': {'email': ...}}]}. Return a list of owner emails, skipping accounts with no owner or no email.
practice_drills.source_title: Stack Overflow Python nested JSON access question
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

extract_owner_emails({'accounts': [{'owner': {'email': 'a@x.com'}}, {'owner': {}}, {}]}) -> ['a@x.com']

extract_owner_emails({'accounts': []}) -> []

extract_owner_emails({}) -> []

practice_drills.title: Flatten CRM contacts
practice_drills.prompt: Write flatten_contacts(records). Each record has 'profile' (name, email, phone) and 'company' (name) dicts. Return a flat list of {'name','company','email','phone'} using None for any missing value.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks How to Parse Nested JSON in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

flatten_contacts([{'profile': {'name': 'Ada', 'email': 'a@x.com', 'phone': '555'}, 'company': {'name': 'Acme'}}]) -> [{'name': 'Ada', 'company': 'Acme', 'email': 'a@x.com', 'phone': '555'}]

flatten_contacts([{'profile': {'name': 'Lin'}}]) -> [{'name': 'Lin', 'company': None, 'email': None, 'phone': None}]

flatten_contacts([]) -> []

practice_drills.title: Find missing required fields
practice_drills.prompt: Write find_missing_emails(data) for data shaped like {'users': [{'id': .., 'contact': {'email': ..}}]}. Return the list of user ids whose contact.email is missing or empty.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks JSON Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

find_missing_emails({'users': [{'id': 1, 'contact': {'email': 'a@x.com'}}, {'id': 2, 'contact': {}}]}) -> [2]

find_missing_emails({'users': [{'id': 3, 'contact': {'email': ''}}]}) -> [3]

find_missing_emails({'users': []}) -> []

lessons.title: Transform JSON Into a Summary
lessons.explanation: Startup integrations often need a compact summary from a noisy JSON response. You will compute totals, top items, and language counts from repository JSON.
lessons.example_code:
import json
payload = json.loads(raw_text)
pretty = json.dumps(payload, indent=2)

guided_exercise.title: Repository report
guided_exercise.prompt: Implement build_repo_report(repos). Return total repos, total stars, top repo, and language counts.

challenge_exercise.title: Sort and aggregate
challenge_exercise.prompt: Extend your report logic to be stable when repos have missing language or zero stars.

reflection_questions: What makes JSON different from a Python dictionary?
What should your code do with null language values?
hints: total_repos is len(repos).
Use sum(repo.get('stargazers_count', 0) for repo in repos).
max(repos, key=lambda repo: repo.get('stargazers_count', 0)) finds the top repo.
Count languages with a dictionary and skip missing languages unless your product needs an Unknown bucket.

practice_drills.title: Load and summarize JSON
practice_drills.prompt: Write summarize_repos(json_string). The string is JSON for a list of repos (each with 'name', 'stars', 'language'). Return {'total': N, 'top': name_with_most_stars, 'languages': {lang: count}}.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks JSON Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Round-trip JSON safely
practice_drills.prompt: Write round_trip(report). Serialize the dict to JSON and parse it back, returning the restored object so it equals the original.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks JSON Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Flatten nested JSON paths
practice_drills.prompt: Write flatten_json(data). Turn a nested dict into a flat dict whose keys are dot paths, e.g. {'user': {'profile': {'name': 'Ada'}}} -> {'user.profile.name': 'Ada'}.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks How to Parse Nested JSON in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

lessons.title: Classify HTTP Outcomes
lessons.explanation: HTTP status codes are the first debugging signal in an integration. Learn to group success, client mistakes, authentication failures, rate limits, and server errors.
lessons.visual: client -> request(headers, params, body) -> server -> response(status, headers, body)
lessons.example_code:
if response.status_code == 429:
    raise RuntimeError('Back off and retry later')

guided_exercise.title: Status classifier
guided_exercise.prompt: Implement classify_status(code): 2xx success, 401/403 auth_error, 429 rate_limited, 4xx client_error, 5xx server_error.

challenge_exercise.title: Explain retry behavior
challenge_exercise.prompt: Add comments or helper logic that distinguishes retryable 429/500 from non-retryable 400/404.

reflection_questions: Why is 401 different from 403?
Which status codes would you retry automatically?
hints: Check the most specific codes before broad ranges.
401 and 403 are both auth-related for this exercise.
429 deserves its own bucket before general 4xx.
Return strings exactly as the validator expects: success, auth_error, rate_limited, client_error, server_error.

practice_drills.title: Map status codes to actions
practice_drills.prompt: Write status_action(code). Map an HTTP status to an action string: 2xx -> 'accept', 429 -> 'rate-limit backoff', 401/403 -> 'refresh credentials', 5xx -> 'retry', everything else -> 'escalate'.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 6 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:
(showing 3; 3 more cases are hidden)

status_action(200) -> 'accept'

status_action(204) -> 'accept'

status_action(429) -> 'rate-limit backoff'

practice_drills.title: Explain 400 vs 404
practice_drills.prompt: Write explain_status(code). Return a customer-facing message: 400 -> 'Bad request: the data sent was invalid.', 404 -> 'Not found: the requested resource does not exist.', otherwise 'Unexpected status: <code>'.
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.example: explain_status(404) -> 'Not found: the requested resource does not exist.'
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

explain_status(400) -> 'Bad request: the data sent was invalid.'

explain_status(404) -> 'Not found: the requested resource does not exist.'

explain_status(500) -> 'Unexpected status: 500'

cases.expected: Bad request: the data sent was invalid.
Not found: the requested resource does not exist.

practice_drills.title: Classify retryable failures
practice_drills.prompt: Write split_retryable(responses) where each response is {'status': code}. Return {'retryable': [...statuses...], 'non_retryable': [...]}. Retryable means 429 or any 5xx.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

lessons.title: Build a GitHub Profile Explorer
lessons.explanation: GET requests power lookups, search, and pagination. You will analyze a GitHub user and repository list like a real customer-facing integration.
lessons.visual:
GET /users/{username} -> user JSON
GET /users/{username}/repos?page=1 -> repo JSON -> profile report
lessons.example_code:
import requests
response = requests.get(url, params={'page': 1, 'per_page': 100})
response.raise_for_status()
data = response.json()

guided_exercise.title: Analyze profile
guided_exercise.prompt: Implement analyze_profile(user, repos). Include username, display name, total stars, most-starred repo, and language breakdown.

challenge_exercise.title: Pagination plan
challenge_exercise.prompt: Add a fetch_all_pages helper that would keep requesting pages until an empty page is returned.

reflection_questions: How would you prevent rate-limit surprises?
What fields should a customer-facing profile explorer show first?
hints: Start by computing total_stars from repos.
max(..., key=...) identifies the most-starred repository.
Count languages with a dictionary and skip missing values.
Return exactly username, display_name, total_stars, most_starred_repo, and language_breakdown.

practice_drills.title: Build query parameters
practice_drills.prompt: Write build_query(params). Return a new dict that drops any key whose value is None, '' or []. Keep everything else (status, page, per_page, sort).
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Fetch paginated results
practice_drills.prompt: Write collect_all(pages). pages is a list of result pages (each a list). Concatenate records page by page and stop at the first empty page, returning the flattened list.
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 4 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:
(showing 3; 1 more case is hidden)

collect_all([[1, 2], [3], []]) -> [1, 2, 3]

collect_all([[1], [2]]) -> [1, 2]

collect_all([[]]) -> []

practice_drills.title: Filter API search results
practice_drills.prompt: Write filter_results(results, language, min_stars). results is a list of {'name','language','stars'}. Return the names whose language matches and stars >= min_stars.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

filter_results([{'name': 'a', 'language': 'Python', 'stars': 5}, {'name': 'b', 'language': 'Go', 'stars': 9}], 'Python', 3) -> ['a']

filter_results([{'name': 'c', 'language': 'Python', 'stars': 1}], 'Python', 3) -> []

filter_results([], 'Python', 0) -> []

practice_drills.title: Paginate an API (Finest Food Outlets)
practice_drills.source_title: Palantir FDSE HackerRank Online Assessment (reported)
practice_drills.verification_note: Modeled on a task reported by candidates for Palantir's Forward Deployed (Software) Engineer interview; verified against public write-ups on 2026-06-08.
practice_drills.prompt: Reported Palantir FDSE 'Finest Food Outlets' HackerRank task. Write fetch_all_outlets(pages). pages is the list of paginated API responses, each {'page','per_page','total_pages','data':[...]}. Return every item from 'data', concatenated across all pages in order.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

fetch_all_outlets([{'page': 1, 'per_page': 2, 'total_pages': 2, 'data': ['A', 'B']}, {'page': 2, 'per_page': 2, 'total_pages': 2, 'data': ['C']}]) -> ['A', 'B', 'C']

fetch_all_outlets([{'page': 1, 'per_page': 5, 'total_pages': 1, 'data': []}]) -> []

fetch_all_outlets([{'page': 1, 'per_page': 1, 'total_pages': 3, 'data': ['x']}, {'page': 2, 'per_page': 1, 'total_pages': 3, 'data': ['y']}, {'page': 3, 'per_page': 1, 'total_pages': 3, 'data': ['z']}]) -> ['x', 'y', 'z']

practice_drills.title: Filter REST records by period (tvseries)
practice_drills.source_title: Palantir REST/API interview task (reported)
practice_drills.verification_note: Modeled on a task reported by candidates for Palantir's Forward Deployed (Software) Engineer interview; verified against public write-ups on 2026-06-08.
practice_drills.prompt: Reported Palantir REST task (jsonmock tvseries). Write find_series(records, start_year, end_year). Each record is {'name','year'}. Return the names whose year is within [start_year, end_year] inclusive.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

find_series([{'name': 'Show A', 'year': 2010}, {'name': 'Show B', 'year': 2020}], 2000, 2015) -> ['Show A']

find_series([{'name': 'Old', 'year': 1999}], 2000, 2015) -> []

find_series([], 2000, 2015) -> []

lessons.title: Create Records Through an API
lessons.explanation: POST sends new data to a server. The practical skill is validating payloads before sending and checking the created response.
lessons.visual: validate payload -> POST /records body={...} -> 201 Created -> parse created record

guided_exercise.title: Submit clean payloads
guided_exercise.prompt: Use MockAPIClient.post in a scratch script to create a record and inspect the 201 response.

challenge_exercise.title: Reject empty payloads
challenge_exercise.prompt: Design a validation step that fails before sending an empty payload.

reflection_questions: What belongs in client-side validation versus server-side validation?
When should POST be idempotent?
hints: A POST body is usually sent with json=payload in requests.
201 means a resource was created.
Check response.text when debugging failed POST calls.
The mock API returns 400 for an empty payload and 201 for a non-empty payload.

practice_drills.title: Validate POST payload
practice_drills.prompt: Write validate_payload(payload, required). Return {'valid': bool, 'missing': [keys]} listing any required keys absent from payload.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Handle 201 Created
practice_drills.prompt: Write created_id(response) for response {'status': code, 'body': {'id': ..}}. Return the created id only when status is 201, otherwise None.
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

created_id({'status': 201, 'body': {'id': 7}}) -> 7

created_id({'status': 400, 'body': {}}) -> None

created_id({'status': 201, 'body': {}}) -> None

practice_drills.title: JSON body vs form body
practice_drills.prompt: Write choose_body(endpoint). Return 'json' when endpoint['content_type'] is 'application/json', otherwise 'data' (form body).
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

choose_body({'content_type': 'application/json'}) -> 'json'

choose_body({'content_type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded'}) -> 'data'

lessons.title: Update Existing Resources
lessons.explanation: PUT usually replaces a resource; PATCH modifies part of it. FDEs need to choose the method that protects customer data.
lessons.visual:
PUT /user/1 body=full-user -> replace
PATCH /user/1 body={title: 'CTO'} -> partial update

guided_exercise.title: Choose PATCH safely
guided_exercise.prompt: Explain in comments when PATCH is safer than PUT for sparse update forms.

challenge_exercise.title: Avoid accidental data loss
challenge_exercise.prompt: Sketch a helper that requires full required fields before a PUT request.

reflection_questions: What customer data could be lost by a bad PUT?
How would you test a partial update?
hints: Use PATCH when only changing a few fields.
Use PUT when you have a full replacement representation.
Tests should assert unchanged fields stay unchanged after PATCH.
A reliable update helper validates required fields before sending full replacement data.

practice_drills.title: Choose PUT or PATCH
practice_drills.prompt: Write choose_update_method(scenario). If scenario['fields_provided'] == 'all' return 'PUT', otherwise return 'PATCH' (partial update is safer when only some fields are present).
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

choose_update_method({'fields_provided': 'all'}) -> 'PUT'

choose_update_method({'fields_provided': 'some'}) -> 'PATCH'

practice_drills.title: Build partial update payloads
practice_drills.prompt: Write build_patch(original, edited). Return a dict containing only the fields whose value changed from original to edited.
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Guard full replacements
practice_drills.prompt: Write guard_put(payload, required). A PUT replaces the whole resource, so return {'allowed': bool, 'missing': [keys]} and only allow when every required field is present.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

lessons.title: Delete and Verify Resources
lessons.explanation: DELETE often returns 204 with no body. The skill is confirming the outcome and handling permission or missing-resource errors clearly.

guided_exercise.title: Handle empty responses
guided_exercise.prompt: Use your status classifier to treat 204 as success.

challenge_exercise.title: Explain forbidden deletes
challenge_exercise.prompt: Design user-facing error messages for 403 versus 404 during deletion.

reflection_questions: Why should clients not expect JSON from every successful response?
When is a soft delete better than a hard delete?
hints: 204 is a success code even though the body is empty.
403 means the caller is known but not allowed.
404 may mean missing or hidden by permissions.
Treat deletion as a workflow: request, classify status, verify when needed.

practice_drills.title: Delete and verify
practice_drills.prompt: Write is_delete_success(status). A DELETE that returns 204 No Content is a success. Return True only for 204, else False.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

is_delete_success(204) -> True

is_delete_success(200) -> False

is_delete_success(404) -> False

practice_drills.title: Explain 403 vs 404 deletes
practice_drills.prompt: Write delete_error_message(status). 403 -> 'Forbidden: you do not have permission to delete this resource.', 404 -> 'Not found: the resource was already deleted or never existed.', otherwise 'Unexpected status: <code>'.
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.example: delete_error_message(403) -> 'Forbidden: you do not have permission to delete this resource.'
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

delete_error_message(403) -> 'Forbidden: you do not have permission to delete this resource.'

delete_error_message(404) -> 'Not found: the resource was already deleted or never existed.'

delete_error_message(500) -> 'Unexpected status: 500'

cases.expected: Forbidden: you do not have permission to delete this resource.
Not found: the resource was already deleted or never existed.

practice_drills.title: Soft delete design
practice_drills.prompt: Write soft_delete(records, target_id). Each record has an 'id'. Remove the record whose id == target_id and also drop any record already flagged {'deleted': True}, returning the remaining active records.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

soft_delete([{'id': 1}, {'id': 2}, {'id': 3, 'deleted': True}], 2) -> [{'id': 1}]

soft_delete([{'id': 1}], 9) -> [{'id': 1}]

soft_delete([], 1) -> []

lessons.title: Protect Secrets With Environment Variables
lessons.explanation: Production integrations should never hard-code secrets. This lesson uses .env and Authorization headers to model API key and bearer token flows.
lessons.example_code:
from dotenv import load_dotenv
import os
load_dotenv()
headers = {'Authorization': f"Bearer {os.getenv('GITHUB_TOKEN')}"}

guided_exercise.title: Authenticated request setup
guided_exercise.prompt: Copy .env.example to .env and inspect fde_api_academy/http_client.py to see how headers are built.

challenge_exercise.title: Missing secret failure mode
challenge_exercise.prompt: Describe how your code should fail when mocks are disabled but an API key is missing.

reflection_questions: Why should .env not be committed?
How is OAuth different from an API key?
hints: Use python-dotenv to load local variables.
Bearer tokens go in the Authorization header.
Fail early with a clear message when a required secret is missing.
The included http_client.py shows both mock and real authenticated request paths.

practice_drills.title: Build auth headers
practice_drills.prompt: Write build_auth_headers(env) where env is a dict of environment variables. Read env['API_TOKEN'] and return {'Authorization': 'Bearer <token>'}; return {} when the token is missing (never hard-code secrets).
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Diagnose auth failures
practice_drills.prompt: Write diagnose_auth(status). 401 -> 'Missing or expired token: re-authenticate and retry.', 403 -> 'Insufficient permissions: request access for this resource.', otherwise 'No auth issue detected.'.
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.example: diagnose_auth(401) -> 'Missing or expired token: re-authenticate and retry.'
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

diagnose_auth(401) -> 'Missing or expired token: re-authenticate and retry.'

diagnose_auth(403) -> 'Insufficient permissions: request access for this resource.'

diagnose_auth(200) -> 'No auth issue detected.'

cases.expected: Missing or expired token: re-authenticate and retry.
Insufficient permissions: request access for this resource.

practice_drills.title: API key safety checklist
practice_drills.prompt: Write redact_secret(token). Return the token with everything but the last four characters masked, e.g. 'abcd1234' -> '****1234'. Tokens of length <= 4 return '****'.
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

redact_secret('abcd1234') -> '****1234'

redact_secret('xy') -> '****'

redact_secret('123456') -> '****3456'

practice_drills.title: Permission Access Intervals
practice_drills.prompt: A system stores permission records as (timestamp, permission), where permission is one of "none", "member", or "admin" and none < member < admin. Given unsorted records and a required permission, return all maximal contiguous timestamp intervals where the user has access. Access means rank(permission) >= rank(required). All timestamps are unique.
practice_drills.source_title: User-provided LeetCode-style FDE access-control prompt
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified from the user-provided LeetCode-style prompt on 2026-06-21; topic aligns with public FDE interview prep themes around permissions, event streams, and access-control workflows.
practice_drills.expected: Return all maximal contiguous access intervals after sorting by timestamp. Graded on hidden cases covering unsorted records, no access, admin-only access, single-point intervals, and already sorted input. Returning lists like [[2, 4]] or tuples like [(2, 4)] is accepted.

lessons.title: GitHub User Analyzer
lessons.explanation: A real FDE task: turn a GitHub account into useful business context. Analyze stars, languages, repositories, and activity signals.
lessons.visual: users + repos + commits + issues -> analyzer -> executive summary

guided_exercise.title: Analyzer core
guided_exercise.prompt: Reuse analyze_profile and add fields you would show in a customer-facing report.

challenge_exercise.title: Commit statistics design
challenge_exercise.prompt: Design a function signature for commit stats that can work with paginated commit data.

reflection_questions: Which GitHub API endpoints would you read first?
How would you cache results to avoid rate limits?
hints: Separate fetching from analysis.
Keep report fields deterministic and testable.
Paginated endpoints need a loop and an exit condition.
The starter analyzer is intentionally pure: user JSON plus repo JSON in, report dict out.

practice_drills.title: Repository language report
practice_drills.prompt: Write repo_report(repos). Each repo has 'name', 'stargazers_count', 'language'. Return {'total_stars': N, 'top_repo': name, 'language_percentages': {lang: percent}} where percent is round(count/total*100).
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Issue triage summary
practice_drills.prompt: Write triage_issues(issues, stale_days). Each issue has 'id', 'label', 'state', 'age_days'. Return {'open_by_label': {label: count}, 'stale': [ids]} counting only open issues and flagging open issues older than stale_days.
practice_drills.source_title: Exponent Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Commit activity snapshot
practice_drills.prompt: Write recent_commit_counts(commits, days). Each commit has 'author' and 'days_ago'. Return {author: count} counting only commits with days_ago < days.
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

lessons.title: Shape API Data for Dashboards
lessons.explanation: APIs provide records; users need summaries. Practice filtering, mapping, sorting, grouping, and aggregation into dashboard-ready data.
lessons.visual: raw records -> filter active -> map fields -> group by language -> sort by stars
lessons.example_code: top = sorted(repos, key=lambda repo: repo.get('stars', 0), reverse=True)[:5]

guided_exercise.title: Aggregate repositories
guided_exercise.prompt: Extend build_repo_report with any extra grouped summary you find useful.

challenge_exercise.title: Dashboard rows
challenge_exercise.prompt: Return stable, sorted rows that a UI could render without additional logic.

reflection_questions: Which transformations belong in the backend?
How do sorted outputs make tests easier?
hints: Filter first when downstream logic should ignore records.
Map records into a smaller shape before exporting.
Group with a dictionary keyed by category.
Make output ordering deterministic so reports and tests are stable.

practice_drills.title: Filter-map-sort pipeline
practice_drills.prompt: Write pipeline(accounts). Each account has 'id','name','usage','active'. Return only active accounts as {'id','name','usage'}, sorted by usage descending.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks How to Parse Nested JSON in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

pipeline([{'id': 1, 'name': 'a', 'usage': 5, 'active': True}, {'id': 2, 'name': 'b', 'usage': 9, 'active': True}, {'id': 3, 'name': 'c', 'usage': 7, 'active': False}]) -> [{'id': 2, 'name': 'b', 'usage': 9}, {'id': 1, 'name': 'a', 'usage': 5}]

pipeline([]) -> []

practice_drills.title: Group by customer segment
practice_drills.prompt: Write group_by_plan(records). Each record has 'plan' and 'usage'. Return {plan: {'count': N, 'total_usage': U}}.
practice_drills.source_title: DataDriven SQL GROUP BY Interview Reference
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Find outliers
practice_drills.prompt: Write find_outliers(records, threshold). Each record has 'endpoint' and 'p95'. Return the endpoints whose p95 is strictly above threshold.
practice_drills.source_title: Exponent Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

find_outliers([{'endpoint': '/a', 'p95': 120}, {'endpoint': '/b', 'p95': 80}], 100) -> ['/a']

find_outliers([], 50) -> []

practice_drills.title: Portfolio valuation with carry-forward
practice_drills.source_title: Palantir technical phone screen (reported)
practice_drills.verification_note: Modeled on a task reported by candidates for Palantir's Forward Deployed (Software) Engineer interview; verified against public write-ups on 2026-06-08.
practice_drills.prompt: Reported Palantir phone-screen task: portfolio valuation with carry-forward prices. Write portfolio_value(prices, holdings, date). prices is [{'date','symbol','price'}] (sparse, ISO dates); holdings is {symbol: shares}; date is the valuation date. For each holding use the most recent price on or before date (0 if none) and return the total value.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

portfolio_value([{'date': '2024-01-01', 'symbol': 'AAPL', 'price': 100}, {'date': '2024-01-03', 'symbol': 'AAPL', 'price': 110}], {'AAPL': 2}, '2024-01-02') -> 200

portfolio_value([{'date': '2024-01-05', 'symbol': 'MSFT', 'price': 50}], {'MSFT': 3}, '2024-01-01') -> 0

portfolio_value([{'date': '2024-01-01', 'symbol': 'A', 'price': 10}, {'date': '2024-01-02', 'symbol': 'B', 'price': 20}], {'A': 1, 'B': 2}, '2024-01-02') -> 50

lessons.title: Export API Data to CSV
lessons.explanation: Many FDE wins are simple: pull data from an API and hand someone a clean CSV they can trust.
lessons.visual: JSON list -> selected fields -> csv.DictWriter -> report.csv
lessons.example_code:
import csv
with open('report.csv', 'w', newline='') as f:
    writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=['name', 'stars'])
    writer.writeheader()

guided_exercise.title: JSON to CSV plan
guided_exercise.prompt: Create a scratch export script that writes repository name, stars, and language.

challenge_exercise.title: Report contract
challenge_exercise.prompt: Define the exact CSV columns before writing rows, then validate the exported file manually.

reflection_questions: Why is a fixed column contract useful?
What should happen when a value contains a comma?
hints: Use csv.DictWriter instead of joining strings.
Always write a header row.
newline='' avoids extra blank lines on some platforms.
Keep export code separate from API fetch code so it is easy to test.

practice_drills.title: JSON list to CSV rows
practice_drills.prompt: Write to_csv_rows(records, columns). Return a list of rows (lists) where the first row is the column headers and each value is str(rec.get(col, '')) so missing optional fields become blank.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks Convert JSON to CSV in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

to_csv_rows([{'name': 'Ada', 'email': 'a@x.com'}, {'name': 'Lin'}], ['name', 'email']) -> [['name', 'email'], ['Ada', 'a@x.com'], ['Lin', '']]

to_csv_rows([], ['a']) -> [['a']]

practice_drills.title: Nested JSON export
practice_drills.prompt: Write flatten_for_csv(users). Each user has 'profile' (name, email) and 'contact' (phone). Return a flat list of {'name','email','phone'} using None for missing values.
practice_drills.source_title: Stack Overflow JSON to CSV Python question
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

flatten_for_csv([{'profile': {'name': 'Ada', 'email': 'a@x.com'}, 'contact': {'phone': '555'}}]) -> [{'name': 'Ada', 'email': 'a@x.com', 'phone': '555'}]

flatten_for_csv([{'profile': {'name': 'Lin'}}]) -> [{'name': 'Lin', 'email': None, 'phone': None}]

practice_drills.title: CSV escaping edge cases
practice_drills.prompt: Write csv_escape(value). If the string contains a comma, double-quote, or newline, wrap it in double quotes and double any internal quotes. Otherwise return it unchanged.
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks Convert JSON to CSV in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 4 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:
(showing 3; 1 more case is hidden)

csv_escape('a,b') -> '"a,b"'

csv_escape('he said "hi"') -> '"he said ""hi"""'

csv_escape('plain') -> 'plain'

lessons.title: Store API Data Locally
lessons.explanation: SQLite is perfect for local integration prototypes: durable storage, SQL queries, and no separate server.
lessons.visual: API records -> normalize -> INSERT rows -> SELECT grouped report
lessons.example_code: conn.execute('create table if not exists repositories (name text, stars integer, language text)')

guided_exercise.title: Repository database
guided_exercise.prompt: Implement build_repository_database(conn, repos). Create a repositories table and insert rows.

challenge_exercise.title: Aggregate with SQL
challenge_exercise.prompt: Add a query helper that returns total stars by language.

reflection_questions: What schema changes would you need for multiple users?
When should you use INSERT OR REPLACE?
hints: Create the table before inserting.
Use parameterized SQL with ? placeholders.
Commit after inserting rows.
The validator queries sum(stars) grouped by language, so the table and inserted values must match that contract.

practice_drills.title: Insert API records
practice_drills.prompt: Write insert_records(records). Each record has 'name' and 'stars'. Create an in-memory SQLite table, insert the rows with a parameterized query, then return the names sorted alphabetically.
practice_drills.source_title: SQLite Tutorial GROUP BY
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

insert_records([{'name': 'zeta', 'stars': 1}, {'name': 'alpha', 'stars': 2}]) -> ['alpha', 'zeta']

insert_records([]) -> []

practice_drills.title: Aggregate by language
practice_drills.prompt: Write aggregate_by_language(repos). Each repo has 'language' and 'stars'. Return {language: {'count': N, 'stars': total}} (group-by language).
practice_drills.source_title: SQLite Tutorial GROUP BY
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Find duplicate external ids
practice_drills.prompt: Write find_duplicate_ids(records). Each record has 'external_id'. Return {external_id: count} for every id that appears more than once.
practice_drills.source_title: DataDriven SQL GROUP BY Interview Reference
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Session duration per city (SQL join)
practice_drills.source_title: Palantir FDSE HackerRank SQL task (reported)
practice_drills.verification_note: Modeled on a task reported by candidates for Palantir's Forward Deployed (Software) Engineer interview; verified against public write-ups on 2026-06-08.
practice_drills.prompt: Reported Palantir FDSE SQL task 'Client Session Duration Report'. Write session_duration_by_city(cities, clients, sessions). Join clients to cities (client.city_id -> city.id) and sessions to clients (session.client_id -> client.id). Return [{'city','total'}] of total session duration per city, sorted ascending by total then city name.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

session_duration_by_city([{'id': 1, 'name': 'Austin'}, {'id': 2, 'name': 'Boston'}], [{'id': 10, 'city_id': 1}, {'id': 11, 'city_id': 2}], [{'client_id': 10, 'duration': 30}, {'client_id': 11, 'duration': 20}, {'client_id': 10, 'duration': 5}]) -> [{'city': 'Boston', 'total': 20}, {'city': 'Austin', 'total': 35}]

session_duration_by_city([{'id': 1, 'name': 'Solo'}], [{'id': 9, 'city_id': 1}], []) -> []

lessons.title: Diagnose Broken Integrations
lessons.explanation: Debugging is a disciplined loop: reproduce, read the stack trace, isolate the failing assumption, add a narrow fix, and capture a regression test.
lessons.visual: failure -> reproduce -> inspect status/body/trace -> identify assumption -> patch -> test

guided_exercise.title: Missing key resilience
guided_exercise.prompt: Revisit extract_contacts and make it resilient to missing nested dictionaries.

challenge_exercise.title: Root cause report
challenge_exercise.prompt: Write a short debugging note in docs/debugging-notes.md for one failure mode: symptom, cause, fix, test.

reflection_questions: What was the first observable symptom?
Which assumption actually broke?
hints: Start by reproducing the failure with the smallest payload.
Print or inspect the exact object before the failing line.
Replace brittle indexing with explicit validation or safe .get calls.
A strong debugging note separates symptom, root cause, fix, and regression test.

practice_drills.title: Debug missing key
practice_drills.prompt: Write safe_get(data, path). path is a dotted key path like 'user.profile.name'. Walk the nested dict and return the value, or None if any key along the path is missing (no KeyError).
practice_drills.source_title: GeeksforGeeks How to Parse Nested JSON in Python
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

safe_get({'user': {'profile': {'name': 'Ada'}}}, 'user.profile.name') -> 'Ada'

safe_get({'user': {}}, 'user.profile.name') -> None

safe_get({}, 'a.b') -> None

practice_drills.title: Debug bad JSON
practice_drills.prompt: Write parse_response(response) for response {'status','content_type','body'}. If content_type is 'application/json' and body parses, return {'ok': True, 'data': parsed}. Otherwise return {'ok': False, 'status': .., 'content_type': .., 'preview': body[:40]}.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Debug rate limits
practice_drills.prompt: Write backoff_schedule(n). Return the exponential backoff delays for n retries as [1, 2, 4, 8, ...] (i.e. 2**i for i in range(n)).
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 4 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:
(showing 3; 1 more case is hidden)

backoff_schedule(3) -> [1, 2, 4]

backoff_schedule(1) -> [1]

backoff_schedule(0) -> []

practice_drills.title: Fix double-counted contacts
practice_drills.source_title: Palantir debugging interview round (reported)
practice_drills.verification_note: Modeled on a task reported by candidates for Palantir's Forward Deployed (Software) Engineer interview; verified against public write-ups on 2026-06-08.
practice_drills.prompt: Reported Palantir debugging round: fix a double-counting contact-tracing bug. Write count_contacts(pairs). pairs is a list of [a, b] contacts that may be duplicated or reversed. Count each unique undirected contact once (ignore self-pairs) and return {person: number_of_distinct_contacts}.

lessons.title: Read Docs and Build Integration Adapters
lessons.explanation: Every new integration starts with docs. Identify auth, base URL, endpoints, request shape, response shape, errors, and rate limits before coding.
lessons.visual: docs -> endpoint contract -> adapter function -> parser -> tests with mock response

guided_exercise.title: Mocked adapter
guided_exercise.prompt: Use MockAPIClient to fetch github_user or weather data and inspect the response shape.

challenge_exercise.title: Integration checklist
challenge_exercise.prompt: Create an integration checklist for GitHub, JSONPlaceholder, OpenWeather, OpenAI, and Stripe.

reflection_questions: Which docs section do you read first?
How do mocks reduce risk before using real credentials?
hints: Find authentication requirements first.
Capture example responses as fixtures.
Write parser tests against fixtures.
Use real APIs only after the mock path and error handling are working.

practice_drills.title: Read docs into an adapter
practice_drills.prompt: Write build_adapter(doc). Given an API docs dict, return an adapter {'base_url','auth','endpoint','params','fields'} pulled from the matching doc keys (params/fields default to []).
practice_drills.source_title: Educative REST API Interview Questions
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Mock before live integration
practice_drills.prompt: Write count_items(fixture). fixture is a mock response {'body': json_string} where the body decodes to {'items': [...]}. Return the number of items.
practice_drills.source_title: Exponent Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 2 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

count_items({'body': '{"items": [1, 2, 3]}'}) -> 3

count_items({'body': '{"items": []}'}) -> 0

practice_drills.title: Normalize multiple APIs
practice_drills.prompt: Write normalize_event(record). github records {'source':'github','stars':N} -> {'source':'github','metric':'stars','value':N}; weather records {'source':'weather','temp':T} -> {'source':'weather','metric':'temp','value':T}.
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

lessons.title: Build Practical API Automations
lessons.explanation: Automation glues systems together. Good workflows are observable, idempotent, configurable, and clear when they fail.
lessons.visual: schedule/manual trigger -> fetch data -> transform -> store/export/notify -> log result
lessons.example_code:
def run_workflow():
    data = fetch()
    report = transform(data)
    save(report)
    return report

guided_exercise.title: Activity reporter
guided_exercise.prompt: Use the GitHub analyzer output as the basis for a daily activity reporter.

challenge_exercise.title: Sync workflow design
challenge_exercise.prompt: Design an API data sync tool with retry, dedupe, logging, and SQLite storage.

reflection_questions: What makes a workflow safe to rerun?
What logs would help you debug a failed sync?
hints: Separate fetch, transform, store, and notify steps.
Idempotency usually needs stable external IDs.
Retry only failures that are likely temporary.
The capstone project shows a fuller automation architecture.

practice_drills.title: Design an idempotent sync
practice_drills.prompt: Write idempotent_sync(existing_ids, incoming). existing_ids is a list of ids already stored; incoming is a list of records with an 'id'. Return only the records whose id is not already present, so the job is safe to rerun.
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

idempotent_sync([1, 2], [{'id': 1}, {'id': 3}]) -> [{'id': 3}]

idempotent_sync([], [{'id': 5}]) -> [{'id': 5}]

idempotent_sync([1, 2], []) -> []

practice_drills.title: Build retry policy
practice_drills.prompt: Write retry_decision(status). 429 -> 'retry with backoff', 500 -> 'retry', 400 -> 'do not retry', 401 -> 'refresh auth', anything else -> 'escalate'.
practice_drills.source_title: DataCamp REST API Interview Questions and Answers
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 5 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:
(showing 3; 2 more cases are hidden)

retry_decision(429) -> 'retry with backoff'

retry_decision(500) -> 'retry'

retry_decision(400) -> 'do not retry'

practice_drills.title: Automated report workflow
practice_drills.prompt: Write workflow_steps(). Return the ordered automation steps for the morning report job as the list ['fetch', 'store', 'export', 'log'].
practice_drills.source_title: Exponent Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 1 test case. Your function must return exactly these results:

workflow_steps() -> ['fetch', 'store', 'export', 'log']

lessons.title: Startup-Style Interview Tasks
lessons.explanation: FDE interviews reward shipping useful, robust work under ambiguity. Practice framing assumptions, building a small integration, explaining tradeoffs, and debugging quickly.
lessons.visual: ambiguous task -> clarify assumptions -> small working tool -> tests -> explanation

guided_exercise.title: Easy simulation
guided_exercise.prompt: Pull data from a mocked API and generate a summary report.

challenge_exercise.title: Hard simulation
challenge_exercise.prompt: Build the API Operations Dashboard capstone in projects/api_operations_dashboard.py.

reflection_questions: How did you reduce ambiguity?
What would you improve with another hour?
Which failure mode did you handle best?
hints: Clarify inputs, outputs, and success criteria first.
Build a narrow end-to-end path before adding features.
Use mocks and fixtures to make tests deterministic.
A strong final answer explains design, tradeoffs, errors handled, and how to run it.

practice_drills.title: Customer data report simulation
practice_drills.prompt: Write executive_summary(records). Each record has 'customer' and 'usage'. Return {'total_customers': N, 'total_usage': sum, 'top_customer': name_with_highest_usage}.
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.

practice_drills.title: Broken integration simulation
practice_drills.prompt: Write diagnose_integration(response) for {'status', 'json_valid', 'retries'}. Return a sorted list of issue tags: add 'auth' when status is 401, 'parsing' when json_valid is False, and 'retry' when retries is 0.
practice_drills.source_title: Exponent Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 3 test cases. Your function must return exactly these results:

diagnose_integration({'status': 401, 'json_valid': False, 'retries': 0}) -> ['auth', 'parsing', 'retry']

diagnose_integration({'status': 200, 'json_valid': True, 'retries': 2}) -> []

diagnose_integration({'status': 200, 'json_valid': False, 'retries': 1}) -> ['parsing']

practice_drills.title: System sync design simulation
practice_drills.prompt: Write sync_design_components(). Return the ordered components of a robust CRM<->product sync as ['extract', 'transform', 'load', 'audit_log', 'error_recovery'].
practice_drills.source_title: Blockchain Council Forward Deployed Engineer Interview Guide
practice_drills.verification_note: Verified as a public interview/prep source for this topic on 2026-06-07; prompt wording is adapted for FDE API Academy practice.
practice_drills.expected:
Graded on 1 test case. Your function must return exactly these results:

sync_design_components() -> ['extract', 'transform', 'load', 'audit_log', 'error_recovery']
